hook: You already know that a function takes an input and produces an output — like plugging a number into a formula and getting a result. But what happens when you need to combine, reverse, or even chain multiple functions together to model something real, like the spread of a virus or the path of a thrown ball? This unit gives you the full toolkit for thinking about functions as flexible, powerful objects you can build with.
concept: Every topic here is about treating functions as building blocks — you can inspect them, reshape them, combine them, and invert them, all in service of describing real-world relationships precisely. Formally, this is the study of function algebra and transformation within the framework of the function as a mapping between sets.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Review of Function Notation, Domain, and Range
topic_roadmap.description: You will sharpen your ability to read and write function notation like $f(x)$, identify exactly which inputs a function can accept (domain), and determine all possible outputs (range) — building the precise language you'll need for every topic ahead.

Master the core library of parent functions (such as $x^2$, $|x|$, and $\sqrt{x}$) and learn how shifts, stretches, reflections, and compressions transform their graphs so you can sketch or interpret any related function quickly.
Apply arithmetic operations directly to functions — forming $(f+g)(x)$, $(fg)(x)$, and more — and determine the domain of each new combined function with confidence.

You will learn to chain functions together as $f(g(x))$, interpret what that means in context, and carefully track how the domain restricts the composition — a skill essential for calculus and modeling.

You will discover how to reverse a function's process by finding its inverse $f^{-1}(x)$, verify the relationship using composition, and understand graphically why an inverse reflects across the line $y = x$.

Apply your full function toolkit to construct and interpret mathematical models for real-world scenarios — translating verbal descriptions into equations and using those equations to make meaningful predictions.

You will explore how expressing $x$ and $y$ separately as functions of a third variable $t$ — writing $(x(t),\, y(t))$ — unlocks the ability to describe curved paths and motion that ordinary function notation cannot capture.
